title: W03.Deck.fins3646
body:
FINS3646: Toolkit for Finance
Week 3: Lecture


Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin

UNSW




                                    1
 Overview
 Overview




    Downloading course code
    Data structures overview
       Lists, Tuples, Sets, and Dictionaries




                                                2
 Downloading course code
 Downloading files from Dropbox

 Use the tk_utils module to download and update the code shared in class
        In the PyCharm Console, type:
    1      >>> import tk_utils
    2      >>> tk_utils.sync_dbox()
    3      >>> tk_utils.copy_new_files()

        What each line does:
         1. Import the tk_utils module
         2. Download all files from Dropbox into a local zip archive:
                  toolkit/
                  |__ _dropbox/
                  |   |__ __PLEASE_DO_NOT_MODIFY_THIS_FOLDER   <- Empty marker file
                  |   |__ toolkit.zip    <- Zip file with the shared folder contents

         3. Check for new files inside toolkit.zip that are not yet in your toolkit project folder
                 • The program will list the new files and ask if you want to copy them
                 • Type "yes" to add them to your project. If PyCharm tries to auto-correct your input, press
                   <backspace> and re-enter it.

        Notes:
           There is no need to unzip the toolkit.zip file manually
           copy_new_files will never overwrite your existing files
           If you want to replace a file, move your old copy elsewhere and then run copy_new_files again
           See help(tk_utils.copy_new_files) for more information                                              3
 Code for today




 The following code will be downloaded from Dropbox
    toolkit/
    |__ lectures/
    |   | ...
    |   |__ w02_yf_example0.py
    |   |__ w02_yf_example1.py
    |   |__ w03_lists0.py
    |   |__ w03_lists1.py
    |   |__ w03_lists2.py
    |   |__ w03_sets0.py
    |   |__ w03_dicts0.py
    |   | ...
    |
    |__ toolkit_paths.py           <- Directly under `toolkit`




                                                                 4
 The toolkit_paths module


 The toolkit_paths module includes the locations of important files and folders

    toolkit/
    |__ toolkit_paths.py          <- Locations of files and folders

 Notes:
     This module defines constants pointing to folders inside toolkit:
           toolkit/                     <- toolkit_paths.TOOLKIT_DIR
           |__ data/                    <- toolkit_paths.DATA_DIR
           |__ exercises/               <- toolkit_paths.EXERCISES_DIR
           |__ projects/                <- toolkit_paths.PROJECTS_DIR
           |__ lectures/                <- toolkit_paths.LECTURES_DIR

     It relies on a module called pathlib
           Introduced today, discussed in detail later
     Please do not modify this file
     See lectures/w02_yf_example1.py for an example


                           Let’s take a look at the contents of toolkit_paths.py


                                                                                   5
 Data structures: Introduction
 Data Structures
 Data Structures




 Python provides a number of containers in its standard library, called data structures

      Data structures are collections of Python objects
      We will focus on four commonly used data structures:


          list: ordered and mutable (changeable) collections
          tuple: ordered and immutable (unchangeable) collections
          set: unordered, mutable collection of unique elements
          dictionary: mutable mappings of keys to values, insertion-ordered in the current implementation


          Data structures provide different ways to organise and access Python objects,
                        forming the backbone of most algorithms used in Finance.




                                                                                                             6
 Data Structures: Lists
 Lists




  A list is an ordered collection of Python objects.

  Lists have the following key properties:
        Ordered:
           Objects appear in a fixed order
           This order is set by the programmer and not determined by the values of the objects.
        Mutable:
           Objects may be added to or removed from the list.
           Objects in the list may be replaced.


        Elements in the list may be any Python object, and lists can contain multiple types of objects.




                                                                                                          7
 Data Structures: Lists - Construction




 Lists are typically constructed using literals of the form:
                                     [ expression1, expression2, ...]

      An opening square bracket [
      Zero or more expressions, separated by commas
      A closing square bracket ]


 Examples:
     # A list of the first three integers
     first_three = [1, 2, 3]

     # An empty list
     empty_list = []




                                                                        8
 Data Structures: Lists - Construction




 You are free to mix types within the list:
    lst = [1, "string", True, None]
    print(type(lst))
    # <class 'list'>



 Lists may contain any objects, even other lists:
    lst_a = [1, "string", True, None]
    lst_b = ["a" , lst_a]
    print(lst_b)
    # ['a', [1, 'string', True, None]]




                                                    9
 Data Structures: Lists - Indexing




 We use square brackets to indicate that we want to retrieve an object from a list.

      Python uses zero-based indexing: the initial object is at index 0.
      Each subsequent object in a list is indexed by the next relevant integer.
      Indexing beyond the last object of a list will result in an IndexError.

     example = [1, "string", True, None]
     example[0] # 1
     example[1] # "string"
     example[2] # True
     example[3] # None
     example[4] # IndexError




                                                                                      10
 Data Structures: Lists - Indexing




 You may also index the list “backwards” from the last object to the first using negative numbers.

      The last object of the list is given index -1.
      Each previous object is indexed by the next negative integer.
      Negative indexing beyond the first object of a list will result in an IndexError.
     example[-1] # None
     example[-2] # True
     example[-3] # "string"
     example[-4] # 1
     example[-5] # IndexError




                                                                                                     11
 Data Structures: Lists - Slices




 Slices are used to access subsets of the list. They are defined using the following syntax:

      The name of the source list
      An initial square bracket [
      The index of the start object in the list (“start index”)
      The index of the stop object in the list (“stop index”)
      A closing square bracket ]

     six_letters = ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f"]
     six_letters[1:4] # ["b", "c", "d"]




 Slices include the object at the start index, but do not include the object at the stop index. Negative indices
                                                   may be used.




                                                                                                                   12
 Data Structures: Lists - Slices




 One needs to be careful when slicing lists:

      This decision to take a slice up to, but not including, the final index is counter-intuitive to
      new programmers (and some experienced programmers as well)
      Remember that the length of the slice is the difference between the start and end indices.
      The starting object must occur before the ending object in the list.


 Examples:
     six_letters = ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f"]

     six_letters[-5:-2])      # ['b', 'c', 'd']
     six_letters[1:-2])       # ['b', 'c', 'd']
     six_letters[4:1])        # []




                                                                                                        13
 Data Structures: Lists - Slices




 Python has shorthand for slicing lists from the beginning or to the end:

      A slice from that starts at the first element may omit the first index: lst[:4] is equivalent
      to lst[0:4]
      Slicing up to and including the last element is achieved by omitting the last index: lst[1:]
      begins at the element at index 1 and runs through and including the last element.
      Unlike indexing, slices using endpoint locations before the first element of the list or
      beyond the end of a list do not raise errors.


 Examples:
     six_letters = ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f"]

     six_letters[:-2]          # ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd']
     six_letters[:0]           # []
     six_letters[3:]           # ['d', 'e', 'f']
     six_letters[3:100]        # ['d', 'e', 'f']
     six_letters[-6:100]       # ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f']




                                                                                                      14
 Data Structures: Lists - Reassigning objects




 The object referenced at any location in a list may be reassigned by placing the list and index location on
 the left-hand side of an assignment statement:


 Example:
     lst = ["a", "b", "c"]
     lst[1] = True
     # lst is now ["a", True, "c"]




                                                                                                               15
 Data Structures: Lists Methods - Introduction




 Lists are instances of the list class, which provides many useful methods

 List methods differ in how they act on the list instance
      Methods that modify the list in place
          Change the instance itself
          May or may not produce any further information about the list
          Methods that do not produce any further information will return None
      Methods that do not modify the list in place
          Do not change the instance
          Typically, return list elements or some information about the list


                    Understanding these categories helps you predict
                             method behaviour and avoid common mistakes.




                                                                                  16
 Data Structures: Lists – Methods that Modify in Place




 Many list methods modify the list instance in place

 lst.append(x) -> None
 Add an object to the end of the list (modifies lst returning None)

 Example
     lst = [1, 2]
     lst.append(3)
     print(lst)    # [1, 2, 3]



         The .append method returns None, so it does not produce any information about the list




                                                                                                  17
 Data Structures: Lists – Methods that Modify in Place




 Many list methods modify the list instance in place

 lst.extend(other_lst) -> None
 Add all objects from other_lst to the end of lst (modifies lst returning None)
     lst_a = [1]
     lst_b = [2, 3]
     lst_a.extend(lst_b)
     # lst_a now is [1, 2, 3]



         The .extend method returns None, so it does not produce any information about the list




                                                                                                  18
 Data Structures: Lists – Methods that Modify in Place




 Many list methods modify the list instance in place

 lst.insert(i, x) -> None
 Add a new element at a specific location (modifies lst returning None)

      i: The element location you would like the new object to be inserted.
      x: The object to insert
     lst = [1, True, None]
     lst.insert(1, "string")
     # lst is now [1, "string", True, None]



         The .insert method returns None, so it does not produce any information about the list




                                                                                                  19
 Data Structures: Lists – Methods that Modify in Place




 Many list methods modify the list instance in place

 lst.pop() -> object
 Remove the last object in a list (Modifies lst returning removed object)

 lst.pop(i) -> object
 Remove the object at index i (Modifies lst returning removed object)
     fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

     # Remove and return the last item
     last_fruit = fruits.pop()
     print(last_fruit)   # Output: cherry
     print(fruits)       # Output: ['apple', 'banana']

     # Remove and return the item at index 0
     first_fruit = fruits.pop(0)
     print(first_fruit) # Output: apple
     print(fruits)       # Output: ['banana']




                                                                            20
 Data Structures: Lists – Methods that Modify in Place




 Many list methods modify the list instance in place

 lst.remove(x) -> None
 Eliminates the first object in the list with value x (Modifies lst returning None)
     fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "banana"]

     # Remove the first occurrence of "banana"
     fruits.remove("banana")
     print(fruits) # Output: ['apple', 'cherry', 'banana']

     # Removing results in an error if `x` is not in the list
     fruits.remove("orange") # Raises ValueError: list.remove(x): x not in list




                                                                                      21
 Data Structures: Lists – Methods that Modify in Place




 Many list methods modify the list instance in place

 lst.reverse() -> None
 Permanently reverse the elements in a list (Modifies lst returning None)
     items = [42, "banana", 0, True, "apple", 3.14]
     items.reverse()
     # Items is now [3.14, 'apple', True, 0, 'banana', 42]




                                                                            22
 Data Structures: Lists – Methods that Modify in Place




 Many list methods modify the list instance in place

 lst.sort() -> None
 Permanently reorder the elements using a natural ordering (Modifies lst returning None)

      Numbers will be sorted in ascending order
      Strings will be sorted alphabetically, with all capital letters considered to be before
      lower-case letters
      Sorting of mixed data types may require customisation or raise an error.
     numbers = [5, 3, 9, 1]
     numbers.sort()
     print(numbers) # Output: [1, 3, 5, 9]

     words = ["banana", "apple", "cherry"]
     words.sort()
     print(words) # Output: ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

     mixed = [5, "banana", 3.14, "apple"]
     mixed.sort() # Raises TypeError in Python 3




                                                                                                23
 Data Structures: Lists – Methods that Modify in Place



 Methods that modify lists in place include:

      lst.extend(iterable) -> None
      Add all elements from another iterable (e.g. list, tuple, set) to the end.
      lst.sort() -> None
      Sort the list in place (ascending order by default).
      lst.insert(i, obj) -> None
      Insert an object at position i, shifting later elements to the right.
      lst.pop() -> object
      Remove and return the last element of the list.
      lst.pop(i) -> object
      Remove and return the element at position i.
      lst.remove(x) -> None
      Remove the first occurrence of value x from the list.
      lst.reverse() -> None
      Reverse the order of the list in place.

                                  These methods will be discussed in class


                                                                                   24
 Data Structures: Lists – Methods that do not modify in place



 Some methods do not modify the list in place

 Examples
      lst.count(x) -> int
      Return the number of times x appears in the list (does not modify)

          lst = [1, 2, 2, 3]
          print(lst.count(2))        # 2
          print(lst)                 # [1, 2, 2, 3] (unchanged)



      lst.index(x) -> int
      Return the index of the first occurrence of x in the list (does not modify). Raises a ValueError if x is
      not found.

          fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "banana"]

          print(fruits.index("banana"))         # 1
          print(fruits)                         # ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "banana"]
          fruits.index("lemon")                 # ValueError



                                                                                                                 25
 Containers: Lists - Commonly used methods




 Interestingly, the len() method, which determines the size of lists and other containers, is a built-in method,
 not an instance method.

 len(lst) -> int
 Return the length of the list - Not an instance method
     fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
     len(fruits) # 3


    We have used other built-in methods (like print()). We will discuss these when we get to modules.




                                                                                                                   26
 Data Structures: Lists - Commonly used methods




  Python has a rich collection of list methods. While not critical at this point, you may wish to review the
                       official documentation (or use AI) to see what else is available.




                                                                                                               27
 Data Structures: Tuples
 Data Structures: Tuples




 A tuple is an ordered collection of Python objects.

 Tuples have the following key properties:
      Ordered:
          Objects appear in a fixed order
          This order is set by the programmer and not determined by the values of the objects.
      Immutable
          A tuple has a fixed size when it is created, unlike a list, which can change in size.
          Objects may not be added or removed from the list.
          Objects in the list may not be replaced.


           Elements may be any Python object, and tuples can contain multiple types of objects.




                                                                                                   28
 Data Structures: Tuples - Construction




 Tuples are typically constructed using literals of the form:
                                             (object1, object2)

      An opening parenthesis (
      Zero or more objects, separated by commas
      A closing parenthesis )

 Examples:
     # A three-object tuple
     three_tuple = ("word", 5, False)

     # An empty tuple (Both forms are valid)
     empty_tuple = ()
     empty_tuple = tuple()

     # A single object tuple requires a comma after the object
     single_tuple = ("word",)




                                                                  29
 Data Structures: Tuples - Construction




 You are free to mix types within the tuple:
    tup = (1, "string", True, None)
    print(type(tup))
    # <class 'tuple'>


 Tuples may contain any objects, even other tuples:
    tup_a = (1, "string", True, None)
    list_b = ["c", 48]
    tup_c = ("a", tup_a, list_b)
    print(tup_c)
    # ('a', (1, 'string', True, None), ["c", 48])




                                                      30
 Data Structures: Tuples - Indexing




 Indexing single objects of a tuple is supported using the same notation as for lists

      Square bracket zero-based indexing.
      Backward (negative) indexing is supported.
      It is an IndexError to use an index outside the range.

     t = ("word", 5, False)

     # Accessing using indexing
     t[0] # Also t[-3]
     # "word"
     t[1] # Also t[-2]
     # 5
     t[2] # Also t[-1]
     # False

     # Attempting to access an element
     t[5]
     # IndexError: tuple index out of range




                                                                                        31
 Data Structures: Tuples - Slicing




 Tuple slices are supported using the same notation as for lists

      Square bracket zero-based indexing.
      Backward (negative) indexing is supported.
      It is an IndexError to use an index outside the range.
     t = ("word", 5, False)
     t[0:2]
     # ("word", 5)




                                                                   32
 Data Structures: Tuples - Huh?. . . and also why?




 One may wonder why tuples exist:

 Tuples function similarly to lists. . .
      Ordered collections of Python objects
      Identical indexing and slicing syntax

 . . . but seem less powerful.
      Tuples are immutable (can not be changed)
 Tuples may seem weaker than lists, but immutability has advantages:

      Predictability: Contents cannot be altered accidentally
      Hashability: Tuples can be used as keys in dictionaries or elements in sets (discussed later)
      Efficiency: Tuples are lighter and slightly faster than lists
      Packing and unpacking: Tuples support simultaneous assignment of multiple variables




                                                                                                      33
 Data Structures: Tuples - Unpacking




 Tuples support unpacking, an elegant way to get multiple objects out of a tuple without needing to index
 each one individually

 Unpacking works by listing variable names on the left-hand-side of an assignment operator and
 the tuple on the right:
     # Create a tuple with two objects
     tup = (1, 2)
     # unpack the tuple into two variables:
     (a, b) = tup
     # print
     print(f"`a` is {a} and `b` is {b}")




                                                                                                            34
 Data Structures: Tuples - Unpacking




 Python does not care whether you use parentheses when packing (creating) or unpacking tuples.

      Python will act as if any omitted parenthesis are present.
      Python considers the following lines to be equivalent:

                    a, b, c = 1, True, "word"
                    a, b, c = (1, True, "word")
                    (a, b, c) = 1, True, "word"
                    (a, b, c) = (1, True, "word")




                                                                                                 35
 Data Structures: List and Tuple Constructors




 Lists and tuples can also be created using their class constructors

     # Constructing from an iterable
     list("ABC")          # ['A', 'B', 'C']
     tuple([1, 2, 3])     # (1, 2, 3)

     # Empty containers
     list()                   # []
     tuple()                  # ()




            Constructors are especially useful when converting between types or from
                                     generators into concrete containers.




                                                                                       36
 Data Structures: Lists and Tuples - Key Takeaways




 Lists and tuples are both ordered collections of Python objects

      Lists
          Mutable: can grow, shrink, or change contents
          Workhorse for storing data that evolves (e.g. time series, datasets)
      Tuples
          Immutable: fixed once created
          Useful when immutability is required


      Both support indexing and slicing
      Both can hold any type of Python object, even other containers
      Lists and tuples can be created using literals ([...], (...)) or constructors (list(),
      tuple())




                                                                                               37
 Data Structures: Sets
 Data Structures: Sets




 A set is an unordered collection of unique Python objects.

 Sets have the following key properties:
      Unordered:
         Sets do not have a fixed order that can be relied on across different runs, Python versions, or
            operating systems.
      Mutable:
         Objects may be added to or removed from the list.
      Uniqueness:
         An object can only appear once in the set.


 Use Sets instead of Lists when you want to ensure there is only one reference to the object in the collection
                                        and order does not matter.




                                                                                                                 38
 Data Structures: Sets - Construction




 Sets can be constructed using literals of the form:
                                    { expression1, expression2, ...}

      An opening curly brace {
      A comma-separated list of one or more expressions
      A closed curly brace }
 Python ensures that the set contains only one of each element.
     # A set of the first three integers
     {1, 2, 3}
     # {1, 2, 3}

     # A set of the first three integers
     {1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 3, 2, 1}
     # {1, 2, 3}




                                                                       39
 Data Structures: Sets - Construction




 Sets can also be constructed using the set constructor:
                                              set(iterable)


 Examples
     # Construct a set from a list
     set([1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 3, 2, 1])
     # {1, 2, 3}

     # Construct and empty set
     set()
     # set()



 Important: The literal {} will not construct an empty set. To construct an empty set, you must use the set
                                                  constructor




                                                                                                              40
 Data Structures: Sets - Construction




 Sets may only include hashable objects.

      An object is hashable if it has a fixed, unchanging hash value.
      Hash values are integers that summarise the object’s contents.
      Python uses hash values to quickly look up objects in data structures like sets.
      Hash values are distinct from memory locations. A memory location tells you where an
      object is; a hash value tells you something about what an object is.
      Typically, only immutable objects are hashable.


        Example: Lists cannot be elements of a set because they are mutable and cannot be hashed




                                                                                                   41
 Data Structures: Sets - Indexing




 As an unordered group of Python objects, Sets do not support indexing.




                                                                          42
 Data Structures: Sets - Slicing




 As an unordered group of Python objects, Sets do not support slicing.




                                                                         43
 Data Structures: Sets - Commonly used methods



 Sets are instances of the set class, which provides many useful methods

 Like list methods, many set methods will modify the set in place.
 Examples
      my_set.add(x) -> None
      Add element x to the set if it is not already present (Modifies my_set returning None)
          s = set()
          s.add(1) # s = {1}
          s.add(2) # s = {1, 2}
          s.add(2) # s = {1, 2}

      my_set.remove(x) -> None
      Remove element x from the set, throwing a KeyError if x is not present (Modifies my_set returning
      None)
          s = {'a', 'b'}
          s.remove('a') # s = {'b'}
          s.remove('c') # KeyError: 'c'


              You will encounter many other set methods as we progress through the course.



                                                                                                          44
 Data Structures: Sets - Commonly used expressions



 Some key set operations are performed using operators or built-in functions, not methods

 Examples
      x in my_set -> bool
      Check if an element is in a my_set (Not an instance method)
          s = {1, 2, 3}
          1 in s # -> True
          4 in s # -> False

      x not in my_set -> bool
      Check if an element is not in a my_set (Not an instance method)
          s = {1, 2, 3}
          1 not in s # -> False
          4 not in s # -> True

      len(my_set) -> int
      Get the number of elements in a my_set
          s = {1, 2, 3}
          len(s) # -> 3



                                                                                            45
 Data Structures: Dictionaries
 Data Structures: Dictionaries




 Dictionaries are the most complicated data structure in the Python standard library.

      Real dictionaries link words and definitions
      Python dictionaries link key objects to value objects.
          Dictionaries serve as a hybrid between sets and lists.
          Like sets, each key appears once.
          Like lists, we can have a variety of object values.




                                                                                        46
 Data Structures: Dictionaries



 Daily stock prices are a natural fit for a dictionary:

                       | Date         | Price |
                       |--------------|--------|
                       | '2020-01-02' | 7.1600 |
                       | '2020-01-03' | 7.1900 |
                       | '2020-01-06' | 7.0000 |
                       | '2020-01-07' | 7.1000 |
                       | '2020-01-08' | 6.8600 |
                       | '2020-01-09' | 6.9500 |
                       | '2020-01-10' | 7.0000 |
                       | '2020-01-13' | 7.0200 |
                       | '2020-01-14' | 7.1100 |
                       | '2020-01-15' | 7.0400 |




                                                          47
 Data Structures: Dictionaries



 Daily stock prices are a natural fit for a dictionary:

                       | Date         | Price |
                       |--------------|--------|
                       | '2020-01-02' | 7.1600 |
                       | '2020-01-03' | 7.1900 |
                       | '2020-01-06' | 7.0000 |
                       | '2020-01-07' | 7.1000 |
                       | '2020-01-08' | 6.8600 |
                       | '2020-01-09' | 6.9500 |
                       | '2020-01-10' | 7.0000 |
                       | '2020-01-13' | 7.0200 |
                       | '2020-01-14' | 7.1100 |
                       | '2020-01-15' | 7.0400 |



                              Python dictionaries link key objects to value objects.


 Assuming we want use look up stock prices by date:
      Date is the key
      Price is the value


                                                                                       47
 Data Structures: Dictionaries




 A dictionary is an unordered collection of Python key-value pairs.

 Dictionaries have the following key properties:
      Key-value pairs
          Data is stored as pairs: Each key object is associated with a specific value object.
      Unordered:
          The keys do not have a fixed order that can be relied on across different runs, Python versions, or
            operating systems.

      Mutable:
          Key-value pairs may be added to or removed from the dictionary.
          The values associated with a key may be replaced.


      Keys must be hashable objects. Values can be any Python object and do not need to be unique.




                                                                                                                 48
 Data Structures: Dictionaries - Construction




 Dictionaries are typically constructed using literals of the form:
                                   { key1: value1, key2: value2, ...}

      An opening curly brace {
      Zero or more key-value pairs
          Keys and values are linked by a colon
          Key-value pair groups are separated by commas
      A closing curly brace }
 Python will ensure that the keys of the dictionary are unique, using the last value when a key is
 reused.




                                                                                                     49
 Data Structures: Dictionaries - Construction




 Examples
     # A stock price dictionary
     prc_dic = { '2020-01-02': 7.16,
                 '2020-01-03': 7.19,
                 '2020-01-03': 8.00,
               }
     # {'2020-01-02': 7.16, '2020-01-03': 8.0}

     # An empty dictionary using empty braces
     empty_dict = {}
     # An empty dictionary using the
     d = dict()



   A dictionary definition may span multiple lines, which improves readability. End a key-value line with a
                           comma and put the next key-value pair on the line below.




                                                                                                              50
 Data Structures: Dictionaries - Queries



 We look up or “query” each value by its key (or label):.
                                      Query syntax: dictionary[key]
     prc_dic = { '2020-01-02': 7.1600,
                 '2020-01-03': 7.1900,
                 '2020-01-06': 7.0000,
                 '2020-01-07': 7.1000,
                 '2020-01-08': 6.8600,
               }
     # Get the value stored under '2020-01-02'
     prc_dic['2020-01-02']
     # 7.16




                                                                      51
 Data Structures: Dictionaries - Queries



 We look up or “query” each value by its key (or label):.
                                      Query syntax: dictionary[key]
     prc_dic = { '2020-01-02': 7.1600,
                 '2020-01-03': 7.1900,
                 '2020-01-06': 7.0000,
                 '2020-01-07': 7.1000,
                 '2020-01-08': 6.8600,
               }
     # Get the value stored under '2020-01-02'
     prc_dic['2020-01-02']
     # 7.16


 Attempting to access the value under a key that is not in the dictionary gives a KeyError:
     prc_dic['2020-13-13']
     # KeyError: '2020-13-13'

 The query is one-way; we cannot look up the key that corresponds to a value
     prc_dic[7.1600]
     # KeyError: 7.16



                                                                                              51
 Data Structures: Dictionaries - Commonly used methods



 Dictionaries handle adding key-value pairs and updating value pairs as one operation:

      Adding: Add a new key-value pair to a dictionary
      Updating: Changing the value object for a key object that is already in the dictionary.

 Examples
      example_dict[key] = value
      Add or update a single key-value pair (Modifies example_dict. Not an instance method)
          d = {"a": 1}
          d["a"] = 2 # d = {'a': 2}
          d["b"] = 3 # d = {'a': 2, 'b': 3}

      example_dict.update(key:           value, key:       value, ...)       -> None
      Add or update multiple key-value pairs (Modifies example_dict returning None)
          d_update_with_dict = {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
          d_update_with_dict.update({'a': 2, 'c': 3 })
          print(f"After update d_update_with_dict is {d_update_with_dict}")

          d_update_with_list = {'a': 1, 'b': 2 }
          d_update_with_list.update([('a', 3), ('c', 5 )])
          print(f"After update d_update_with_list is {d_update_with_list}")



                                                                                                52
 Data Structures: Dictionaries - Commonly used methods




 Examples
     example_dict.pop(key) -> object
     Remove a key and its associated value (Modifies example_dict returning value object associated with
     key)
         d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2 }
         ret = d.pop('a')
         print(f"After popping 'a', d is now {d}. The value {ret} was returned.")
         # Output: After popping 'a', d is now {'b': 2}. The value 1 was returned.

         d.pop('c')
         # KeyError: 'c'




                                                                                                           53
 Data Structures: Dictionaries - Commonly used methods



 Some key dicionary operations are performed using operators or built-in functions, not methods

 Examples
      x in example_dict -> bool
      Check if an element is a key in example_dict (Not an instance method)
          d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}
          'a' in d # -> True
          'z' in d # -> False




                                                                                                  54
 Data Structures: Dictionaries - Commonly used methods



 Some key dicionary operations are performed using operators or built-in functions, not methods

 Examples
      x in example_dict -> bool
      Check if an element is a key in example_dict (Not an instance method)
          d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}
          'a' in d # -> True
          'z' in d # -> False

      x not in example_dict -> bool
      Check if an element is a key not in example_dict (Not an instance method)
          d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}
          'a' not in d # -> False
          'z' not in d # -> True




                                                                                                  54
 Data Structures: Dictionaries - Commonly used methods



 Some key dicionary operations are performed using operators or built-in functions, not methods

 Examples
      x in example_dict -> bool
      Check if an element is a key in example_dict (Not an instance method)
          d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}
          'a' in d # -> True
          'z' in d # -> False

      x not in example_dict -> bool
      Check if an element is a key not in example_dict (Not an instance method)
          d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}
          'a' not in d # -> False
          'z' not in d # -> True

      len(example_dict) -> int
      Get the number of key-value pairs in example_dict
          d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}
          len(d) # -> 3

 Data Structures: Sets and Dictionaries – Key Takeaways




 Sets provide a simple and efficient way to manage unique elements.

      Ensure uniqueness automatically (no duplicates)
      Allow fast membership checks (in, not in)
      Work only with hashable elements




                                                                      55
 Data Structures: Sets and Dictionaries – Key Takeaways




 Sets provide a simple and efficient way to manage unique elements.

      Ensure uniqueness automatically (no duplicates)
      Allow fast membership checks (in, not in)
      Work only with hashable elements
 Dictionaries store data as key–value pairs, offering fast lookups by key.

      Keys must be hashable
      Values can be any Python object and do not need to be unique
      Keys are unique; assigning a new value to an existing key overwrites the old one
      Provide efficient queries, insertions, and updates by key

 Data Structures: Holders of object references
 The data structures studied so far hold references to objects, not to variables. This
                can be a significant source of confusion and errors.




                                                                                        56
 Data Structures: Holders of object references - Variable names




 Python works with objects referred to by a variable name, not the variable name itself.
     x = 1
     y = x
     x = 2



                             When this code concludes, what is the value of y?

 Data Structures: Holders of object references




 Let’s pause and summarise key points:
     Python works with objects referred to by a variable name, not the variable name itself.
     Python may create only one instance for int, float, str, bool, and None values.

 Data Structures: Holders of object references - Instances for non-primitives




 Python will generally create multiple instances for non-primitive objects, even if the values are identical.
     a = [1, 2, 3]
     b = [1, 2, 3]
     a is b
     a == b

 Data Structures: Holders of object references - Instances for non-primitives


 Python will generally create multiple instances for non-primitive objects, even if the values are identical.
     a = [1, 2, 3]
     b = [1, 2, 3]
     a is b # Object identity test: False
     a == b # Object value equality test: True


                                a                                              b




                               list                                           list




                                1          2           3



  After evaluating line 2, a and b refers to different lists even though the two lists contain the exact same
                                                    elements.

 Data Structures: Holders of object references




 Let’s pause and summarise key points:
     Python works with objects referred to by a variable name, not the variable name itself.
     Python may create only one instance for a primitive value. This holds for all int, float,
     str, and bool. It is also true of None.

     Python will generally create multiple instances for non-primitive objects, even if the
     values are identical.

     b = [1, 2, 3]
     a.append(4)

 Data Structures: Holders of object references - Example 1

     a = [1, 2, 3]
     b = [1, 2, 3]
